ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Putu Piring. The nearest parent is a steamed rice flour cake or rice confectionery. A new FoodOn concept is recommended for this traditional Malay kueh.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based confectionery — steamed rice flour cake with palm sugar (closest parent match; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Putu Piring)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Putu Piring. A01XS used as closest parent for rice-based confectionery.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Putu Piring or equivalent traditional Malay steamed rice cake.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Putu Piring is approximately 4 pieces (100g), typically served warm on a banana leaf with freshly grated coconut.

nutrient_source.title: Putu Piring — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F11090049
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database under food code F11090049. This is an exact match for Putu Piring as a traditional Malay steamed rice cake with palm sugar and coconut. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values supplemented from enriched_json with confidence 0.9 where HPB data was not available.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Rice-Based Foods — University of Sydney GI Database; coarse rice flour, steamed; GI elevated by gelatinised rice starch
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Putu Piring in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 72 is referenced from the closest analogous food: coarse rice flour steamed product. GI is elevated by gelatinised rice starch and the addition of palm sugar (gula melaka). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 39.5) / 100 ≈ 28.4 for a 100g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~65 mg per 100g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Putu Piring contains approximately 65 mg sodium per 100g (4-piece serving), representing about 3% of the 2300 mg daily recommended limit, making it one of the lowest-sodium traditional kueh options and generally suitable for individuals managing hypertension when consumed in standard portions.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72) and significant sugar content (16.8g per 100g) from gelatinised rice starch and gula melaka
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit Putu Piring to 1–2 pieces as an occasional treat, as the high-GI gelatinised rice flour base combined with palm sugar filling produces a glycemic load of approximately 28 per standard serving, which can cause a significant postprandial blood glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from grated coconut (3.0g per 100g) and high refined carbohydrate content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Putu Piring provides 3.0g saturated fat per 100g primarily from freshly grated coconut, and its high refined carbohydrate content (39.5g per 100g) may contribute to elevated triglycerides with frequent consumption; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake to occasional servings and monitor overall dietary saturated fat and carbohydrate load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Putu Piring is a traditional Malay steamed kueh made from coarse rice flour (tepung beras kasar) pressed into small round saucer-shaped moulds with a nugget of gula melaka (palm sugar) hidden in the centre, then steamed until the rice flour sets into a soft, slightly granular cake. When bitten, the molten gula melaka filling flows out, delivering a deep, caramel-sweet, slightly smoky palm sugar flavour that contrasts with the neutral, lightly fragrant rice flour exterior. The cakes are served warm on a small piece of banana leaf and topped with freshly grated coconut that has been lightly salted for a savoury balance. Putu Piring is a beloved street food and tea-time snack in Singapore and Malaysia, strongly associated with traditional Malay kueh stalls, wet markets, and festive occasions such as Hari Raya. Nutritionally, each 100g serving (approximately 4 pieces) provides 192 kcal, 39.5g carbohydrates, 16.8g sugar, 3.5g fat (3.0g saturated from coconut), and 2.2g protein. The glycemic index is high (~72) due to gelatinised rice starch and palm sugar, making it unsuitable for frequent consumption by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium is low at 65 mg per serving. The dish is naturally gluten-free, vegan, halal, and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database under exact food code F11090049 for Putu Piring. This is a primary approved regional source with an exact match. Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from enriched_json at confidence 0.9 where HPB data was unavailable.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Putu Piring is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (food code F11090049) or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-13. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this traditional steamed kueh is not reported in the HPB Foodo database. trans_fat_g is confirmed 0.0 per HPB source. cholesterol_mg is confirmed 0.0 per HPB source (plant-based ingredients only). vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed 0.0 per HPB source. Copper (0.1 mg), manganese (0.35 mg), and vitamin_k (0.0 mg) values are from enriched_json at confidence 0.9 as these micronutrients were not individually reported in the HPB primary source. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest steamed coarse rice flour product; no direct GI measurement for Putu Piring exists in the database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (72 × 39.5) / 100 = 28.44, rounded to 28.4 for a 100g (4-piece) serving. Hindi local name provided as a transliteration/descriptive form as no established Hindi-script culinary term exists for this Malay dish.
